title: Fox-proofing a chicken run
subtitle: The wire types, burial depths, and gate fittings that actually keep foxes out.
excerpt: A fox will try the same weak point in a fence every night until it finds a way through. Standard chicken wire stops nothing, it rusts, stretches, and a fox can chew through it in minutes. Here's what wire to use, how to bury the skirt, and how to check the result.
sourceNotes: RSPCA livestock housing guidance. DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Meat Chickens and Laying Hens. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal extension of fencing wire laid flat on the ground around the outside of a run, which prevents foxes digging at the base.
Welded wire mesh with small square openings, typically 12.5 mm or 25 mm; much stronger than chicken wire and the correct material for predator-proof runs.

content.text: Standard hexagonal chicken wire is a useful deterrent against chickens wandering, but not against foxes. A fox bites through it or pulls it apart at the joins within a few minutes. The materials that actually work are welded wire mesh (
) for the sides and top, with an
at the base to prevent digging. A coop that's completely enclosed with these materials can be left safely overnight and when you're away.

Use welded wire mesh with 12.5 mm openings (half-inch hardware cloth) for walls and roofs. This gauge stops foxes, rats, and mink. Avoid chicken wire for any surface where security matters. For the apron, you can use a lighter gauge, 19 gauge welded mesh is fine at ground level.
Attach a 60 cm wide apron of welded mesh to the base of the run frame.
Lay it flat on the ground extending outward from the run perimeter. Don't fold it down into a trench, a horizontal apron is just as effective as a buried one and is easier to install.
Peg it down with landscaping staples or tent pegs at 30 cm intervals. Cover with a thin layer of soil or turf if you prefer it less visible.

The gate is usually the weakest point. Use a bolt latch that requires two separate actions to open, a simple sliding bolt can be lifted by a fox pawing at the gate. A twist-bolt or double-latch model is better. Check that the gate fits tightly with no gap at the bottom; a fox can squeeze through a surprisingly small space if motivated.

A single strand of electric wire at 20 cm height, charged from a battery unit, run around the outside of a standard chicken-wire run gives good protection without replacing the fencing entirely. Foxes learn quickly and usually avoid an electrified perimeter after the first contact. The battery unit needs checking weekly and the wire needs weeding to prevent shorting.

Walk the perimeter every few weeks looking for: apron corners lifted, wire joints pulled apart, posts leaning, gate gaps widened by ground movement. A fox that tests the perimeter and fails will try again on the same spot next visit.
